food_name: Crab Meat Noodle Soup Special (Bun Rieu Cua Dac Biet)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese crab noodle soup (Bun Rieu Cua). Mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_03311737) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soup, noodle-based, with seafood — approximate match for Vietnamese crab rice vermicelli soup with pork and tofu toppings
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Bun Rieu Cua. Code A0FKP (noodle soup with seafood) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific Vietnamese dish. SNOMED CT not applicable for this food record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bun Rieu Cua Dac Biet weighs approximately 600 g and includes rice vermicelli, crab meat, pork belly, pork ham, tofu, tomato, and a generous serving of crab-tomato broth.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Soup, noodle-based with seafood and pork components; HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food — rice vermicelli and component analogy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bun Rieu Cua Dac Biet exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values were estimated from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entries for crab meat (FDC ID 174202), rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168917), pork belly (FDC ID 168289), firm tofu (FDC ID 172475), and tomato (FDC ID 170457), and HPB Singapore data for similar Vietnamese noodle soups. Values are plausible for a mixed crab-pork noodle soup at the stated portion size. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Rice vermicelli GI analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bun Rieu Cua Dac Biet. GI estimated at 61 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being rice vermicelli, for which the University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 58–65 (mean ~61) for cooked rice vermicelli. The presence of protein (crab, pork, tofu) and fat (pork belly) in the dish is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain rice vermicelli. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 40.02) / 100 ≈ 24.4, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 6.67 × 6 = 40.02 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 366.67 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 2,200 mg sodium per 600 g bowl — approaching or exceeding the daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, request reduced shrimp paste and crab paste in the broth, avoid adding fish sauce or soy sauce at the table, and consider requesting a lower-sodium broth preparation to keep total sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a refined carbohydrate with a medium glycemic index (~61); a full 600 g bowl delivers approximately 40 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~24, which is in the high GL range and requires careful portion management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to half a bowl to reduce glycemic load, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, prioritize the protein-rich toppings (crab meat, tofu) to slow glucose absorption, and avoid sweet condiments or sugar added to the broth.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.0 g per 100 g (12 g per bowl) from pork belly and pork ham, combined with cholesterol at 41.67 mg per 100 g (250 mg per bowl) and high sodium, presents a combined cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request leaner pork cuts or substitute pork belly with extra tofu and crab meat, limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake, and avoid adding high-sodium condiments to keep total sodium within the recommended 1,500–2,000 mg daily limit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly and pork ham are rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids) that amplify pro-inflammatory cytokines; tomato as a nightshade vegetable may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals; high sodium from shrimp paste worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request a pork-free version substituting pork belly and pork ham with extra crab meat and tofu, monitor for joint flare responses to tomato, and request reduced shrimp paste and crab paste to minimize sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún Riêu Cua Đặc Biệt (Special Crab Meat Noodle Soup) is a premium Vietnamese noodle soup dish built on a rich, tangy crab-tomato broth made from simmered pork bones, crab paste, shrimp paste, and ripe tomatoes. The soup is served over soft rice vermicelli (bún) and topped with generous portions of crab meat, pork belly, pork ham, silken or firm tofu, and soft-cooked egg. The broth is characteristically reddish-orange from tomato and annatto, with a savory-umami depth from fermented shrimp paste and crab paste. The texture profile combines silky noodles, tender crab meat, soft tofu, and fatty pork belly. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 108 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 5.83 g fat, and 6.67 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 366.67 mg — making sodium management the primary clinical concern. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load (~2,200 mg per bowl), moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to medium-GI refined carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~24 per serving, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol from pork belly. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is commonly consumed as a main meal in Vietnamese cuisine and is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bun Rieu Cua Dac Biet exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component entries for crab meat, rice vermicelli, pork belly, tofu, and tomato. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation from component analogy rather than a direct dish measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese crab noodle soup dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database as of 2024. No approved source reports these values for Bun Rieu Cua as a prepared dish. trans_fat_g is reported at 0.03 g per 100 g from the input record (ai_generated source); this is a trace level consistent with minor contributions from pork belly fat. All core macros (protein_g 7.5, fat_g 5.83, carbohydrate_g 6.67) are non-zero and consistent with a mixed crab-pork noodle soup. GI estimated at 61 (medium) using rice vermicelli as the analogous food from the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated at approximately 24.4. The dish is not halal due to pork belly and pork ham content. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore were used instead. Chinese local names (simplified: 特别螃蟹米粉汤; traditional: 特別螃蟹米粉湯), Malay (Sup Mi Ketam Istimewa), Tamil (சிறப்பு நண்டு நூடுல்ஸ் சூப்), and Hindi (विशेष केकड़ा नूडल सूप) names added to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for this Vietnamese dish served in Singapore context. These are descriptive translations of the dish name and are provided for retrieval coverage; they are not established culinary terms in those languages.
